title: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Autoimmune Pathogenesis in Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders (PNDs)
items.content: Most PNDs are considered to have an autoimmune pathogenesis triggered by underlying cancers. These disorders often involve immune responses against neuronal proteins ectopically expressed by tumors or altered immunologic responses due to certain tumors like thymomas.
keyPoints: 60% of patients with PNDs experience neurologic symptoms before cancer diagnosis.
PNDs occur in 0.5–1% of all cancer patients, but affect 2–3% of those with neuroblastoma or SCLC and 30–50% with thymoma.
High-risk antibodies (>70% probability of an underlying tumor) target intracellular proteins except Tr (DNER).
Intermediate- and low-risk antibodies (<30% probability) target cell-surface proteins, except GAD65 and GFAP.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Role of T-Cell Mechanisms in PNDs with Intracellular Antigens
items.content: In PNDs associated with antibodies against intracellular antigens, cytotoxic T-cell mechanisms play a predominant role. These involve extensive infiltrates of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, microglial activation, gliosis, and neuronal loss.
keyPoints: T-cell-mediated cytotoxicity is implicated in the pathogenesis of PNDs with intracellular antigens.
Neurologic syndromes associated with these mechanisms are often resistant to therapy.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Immunotherapy Response in PNDs with Cell-Surface Antibodies
items.content: PNDs associated with antibodies to antigens expressed on the neuronal cell surface or at the neuromuscular junction are mediated by direct antibody effects and are more responsive to immunotherapy.
keyPoints: These disorders can occur with or without a cancer association, affecting children and young adults.
Some PNDs may be triggered by viral encephalitis leading to autoimmune encephalitis.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.title: Early Recognition and Tumor Control in Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders (PNDs)
items.content: Recognizing PND early is crucial as symptoms often precede tumor detection, develop rapidly, and prompt tumor control can improve neurologic outcomes.
keyPoints: Symptoms of PNDs may appear before the tumor is known.
Neurologic syndromes in PNDs usually develop rapidly with severe deficits.
Prompt tumor control improves neurologic outcomes.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Utility of MRI/CSF in PNDs
items.content: MRI and CSF studies are essential to rule out direct cancer spread, though they often show nonspecific findings. Antineuronal antibodies aid diagnosis but are not universally present.
keyPoints: MRI findings in PNDs are typically nonspecific; limbic encephalitis may show mesial temporal lobe abnormalities.
CSF profile: mild to moderate pleocytosis (<200 mononuclear cells), increased protein, variable oligoclonal bands.
Antineuronal antibodies present in 60-70% of CNS PNDs and <20% of peripheral nervous system PNDs.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS) and Cancer Screening Protocol
items.content: In patients diagnosed with Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, which is frequently associated with small cell lung cancer (SCLC), a chest and abdomen CT or body PET scan should be performed. If these initial scans are negative, periodic tumor screening should continue for at least 3 years following the neurologic diagnosis.
keyPoints: LEMS often associates with SCLC
Initial imaging: Chest and abdomen CT or body PET scan
If negative, continue periodic tumor screening for at least 3 years
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.title: Hu Antibodies and Paraneoplastic Encephalomyelitis in SCLC Patients
items.content: Patients with small cell lung cancer (SCLC) presenting with paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis or focal encephalitis often have Hu antibodies present in both serum and cerebrospinal fluid. These conditions are typically resistant to treatments targeting intracellular antigens, but approximately 30% of patients with anti-Ma2-associated encephalitis may respond to tumor treatment and immunotherapy.
keyPoints: Hu antibodies commonly found in SCLC patients with paraneoplastic syndromes
Approximately 30% response rate to tumor treatment and immunotherapy in anti-Ma2-associated encephalitis
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.title: Imaging and Antibody Testing in Paraneoplastic Neuropathies
items.content: In paraneoplastic neuropathies, if neuronal antibodies are negative, the diagnosis relies on demonstrating cancer and excluding other neurologic disorders. Combined CT and PET scans can uncover tumors undetected by other tests. For germ cell tumors of the testis or ovarian teratomas, ultrasound (testicular, transvaginal, or pelvic) and MRI or CT of the abdomen and pelvis may reveal hidden tumors.
keyPoints: Negative neuronal antibodies necessitate cancer demonstration
Combined CT and PET scans can detect otherwise undetected tumors
Ultrasound and MRI/CT for germ cell tumors and ovarian teratomas
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.title: NMDAR Antibody Encephalitis: Clinical Presentation and Management
items.content: NMDAR antibody encephalitis typically affects young women and children, presenting with a prodrome resembling viral illness followed by severe psychiatric symptoms, sleep dysfunction, memory loss, seizures, and autonomic instability. Diagnosis may be delayed due to misdiagnosis as other conditions like viral encephalitis or neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
keyPoints: Occurs in young women and children; men and older patients can also be affected.
Symptom progression includes prodrome, psychiatric symptoms, sleep dysfunction, memory loss, seizures, decreased consciousness, abnormal movements, autonomic instability, hypoventilation.
Monosymptomatic psychosis occurs in about 5% of patients.
Clinical relapses occur in 12–24% during the first 2 years after initial presentation.
Intrathecal synthesis of antibodies is common; brain MRI normal in ~65%, FLAIR abnormalities in 35%.
Associated teratoma: 46% in females ≥12, <7% in girls <12; rare in young males. Tumors more frequent (>20%) in patients >45 years.
Treatment with first-line immunotherapy (steroids, IVIg, plasma exchange) and second-line if needed (rituximab, cyclophosphamide).
85–90% of patients show substantial neurologic improvement or full recovery.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.title: Autoimmune Encephalitis Post-Herpes Simplex Infection
items.content: Approximately 25% of patients with herpes simplex encephalitis develop autoimmune encephalitis, characterized by abnormal movements in children and cognitive/psychiatric symptoms in adults. This condition arises weeks after the viral infection resolves and is associated with new antibody synthesis against NMDAR and other neuronal proteins.
keyPoints: 25% of patients post-herpes simplex encephalitis develop autoimmune encephalitis.
Children exhibit choreoathetosis; adults show cognitive/psychiatric symptoms.
Develops weeks after viral infection resolution.
Associated with new antibody synthesis against NMDAR and other neuronal proteins.
Less responsive to immunotherapy compared to anti-NMDAR encephalitis.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.title: AMPA Receptor Antibody Encephalitis: Characteristics and Tumor Association
items.content: AMPA receptor antibody encephalitis primarily affects middle-aged women, presenting with acute limbic dysfunction or psychiatric symptoms. A significant proportion (70%) of these patients have an underlying tumor in the lung, breast, or thymus.
keyPoints: Affects middle-aged women.
Presents with acute limbic dysfunction or psychiatric symptoms.
70% of patients have an underlying tumor (lung, breast, thymus).
Brain MRI shows typical features of limbic encephalitis in ~50% of cases.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.title: GABA_A Receptor Antibody Encephalitis: Seizures and MRI Findings
items.content: Encephalitis with GABA_A receptor antibodies is characterized by prominent seizures, often requiring a pharmacologically induced coma. In approximately 80% of patients, brain MRI shows multifocal, asynchronous, cortical-subcortical T2/FLAIR abnormalities predominantly involving temporal and frontal lobes.
keyPoints: Prominent seizures and status epilepticus
Approximately 80% show specific MRI findings: multifocal, asynchronous, cortical-subcortical T2/FLAIR abnormalities
Predominantly involves temporal and frontal lobes, but also basal ganglia and other regions
Most patients do not have an underlying tumor; some may have thymoma
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.title: LGI1 Antibody Encephalitis: Clinical Presentation and Association with HLA
items.content: Encephalitis with LGI1 antibodies often presents in patients older than 50 years, characterized by short-term memory loss, seizures, hyponatremia, and sleep dysfunction. MRI frequently shows increased FLAIR signal in medial temporal lobes. An association with specific HLA haplotypes has been identified.
keyPoints: Predominates in patients >50 years (65% male)
Characterized by short-term memory loss, seizures, hyponatremia, sleep dysfunction
MRI often shows increased FLAIR signal in medial temporal lobes
<40% preceded by faciobrachial dystonic seizures
15% present with rapidly progressive cognitive decline
Less than 5% have thymoma
Association with HLA haplotypes DRB1'07:01, DQB1'02:02, DQA1'02:01, and DRB4
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.title: Caspr2 Antibody Encephalitis: Clinical Features and Thymoma Association
items.content: Encephalitis with Caspr2 antibodies is associated with encephalopathy, cerebellar symptoms, peripheral nervous system hyperexcitability, dysautonomia, insomnia, neuropathic pain, and weight loss. Morvan syndrome patients usually have Caspr2 antibodies, with a higher thymoma association in these cases.
keyPoints: Associated with encephalopathy, cerebellar symptoms, peripheral nervous system hyperexcitability, dysautonomia, insomnia, neuropathic pain, weight loss
Morvan syndrome includes confusion, hallucinations, sleep dysfunction, autonomic alterations, and peripheral nerve hyperexcitability
About 20% of patients with Caspr2 antibody-associated syndromes have thymoma
Higher (~50%) in patients with Morvan syndrome
Association with HLA-DRB1^11.01
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.title: Anti-IgLON5 Disease: Clinical Features and Treatment Response
items.content: Anti-IgLON5 disease is a chronic encephalopathy associated with REM and NREM parasomnia, obstructive sleep apnea, bulbar symptoms, movement disorders, oculomotor dysfunction, and cognitive decline in less than half of cases. It occurs predominantly in the early 60s, affecting men and women equally. The disorder shows strong HLA associations but is not linked to cancer. Treatment response is generally poor, with some neuropathologic findings suggesting neuronal tauopathy.
keyPoints: Characterized by REM/NREM parasomnia, obstructive sleep apnea, bulbar symptoms (dysphagia, stridor), unsteadiness, movement disorders, oculomotor dysfunction
Median age of onset in early 60s; equal gender distribution
Strong association with HLA-DRB1*10:01 and HLA-DQB1*05 haplotypes (present in 60% of patients)
Brain MRI usually normal or minor changes; CSF may show transient lymphocytic pleocytosis
Not associated with cancer; poor response to immunotherapy
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.title: MOG Antibody Syndromes: Clinical Presentation and Treatment Response
items.content: MOG antibody-associated syndromes often present in children and young adults with optic neuritis, myelitis, brainstem or cerebellar symptoms, or ADEM. Most patients respond to immunotherapy, though relapses occur in about 30%. Unilateral or bilateral cortical encephalitis is less common, with variable treatment response. MOG antibody syndromes are not typically associated with tumors.
keyPoints: Commonly affects children and young adults
Symptoms include optic neuritis, myelitis, brainstem/cerebellar symptoms, ADEM
85% respond to immunotherapy; 30% experience relapses
Unilateral/bilateral cortical encephalitis with variable response
Not associated with tumors
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.title: Paraneoplastic Cerebellar Degeneration: Clinical Features and Treatment Outcomes
items.content: Paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration often begins with dizziness, oscillopsia, blurry vision, nausea, vomiting, followed by dysarthria, ataxia, and dysphagia. MRI may show cerebellar atrophy later in the disease course. It is associated with tumors like SCLC, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, and Hodgkin's lymphoma. Anti-Yo antibodies are linked to cerebellar degeneration in breast or gynecologic cancers. Treatment response varies; some surface antigen-associated syndromes respond better to immunotherapy.
keyPoints: Prodrome includes dizziness, oscillopsia, blurry vision, nausea, vomiting
Progression to dysarthria, ataxia, dysphagia
MRI may show cerebellar atrophy later
Associated with SCLC, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, Hodgkin's lymphoma
Anti-Yo antibodies linked to cerebellar degeneration in certain cancers
Variable treatment response; better outcomes for surface antigen-associated syndromes
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD
items.title: Opsoclonus-Myoclonus Syndrome: Recognizing Paraneoplastic Associations and Treatment Approaches
items.content: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome can be paraneoplastic, often linked to lung and breast cancers in adults and neuroblastoma in children. The pathogenesis may involve disinhibition of the cerebellar fastigial nucleus. Ri antibodies are associated with a subset of these cases, presenting additional symptoms like muscle rigidity and autonomic dysfunction. Treatment focuses on addressing the underlying tumor and using immunotherapy.
keyPoints: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome can be paraneoplastic, linked to lung and breast cancers in adults, neuroblastoma in children.
Disinhibition of the cerebellar fastigial nucleus is suggested as a pathogenic mechanism.
Ri antibodies are associated with muscle rigidity, laryngeal spasms, and autonomic dysfunction in some cases.
Treatment includes tumor management and immunotherapy (glucocorticoids, plasma exchange, IVIg).
At least 50% of children with opsoclonus-myoclonus have an underlying neuroblastoma.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD
items.title: Paraneoplastic Spinal Cord Syndromes: Clinical Presentation and Prognosis
items.content: Paraneoplastic spinal cord syndromes, such as subacute motor neuronopathy and acute necrotizing myelopathy, may present with upper or lower motor neuron dysfunction. These conditions are often associated with breast and lung carcinomas and can be linked to CRMP5 or amphiphysin antibodies. MRI findings typically show longitudinally extensive, symmetric abnormalities in the spinal cord. The prognosis is generally poor.
keyPoints: Paraneoplastic spinal cord syndromes include subacute motor neuronopathy and acute necrotizing myelopathy.
Associated with breast and lung carcinomas, CRMP5 or amphiphysin antibodies.
MRI shows longitudinally extensive, symmetric abnormalities in the spinal cord.
Prognosis is poor.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME
items.title: Identify Paraneoplastic Stiff-Person Syndrome: Antibody Associations and Treatment Strategies
items.content: Paraneoplastic stiff-person syndrome is characterized by muscle rigidity, stiffness, and painful spasms that improve with sleep and general anesthetics. Electrophysiologic studies show continuous motor unit activity. The presence of amphiphysin antibodies suggests a paraneoplastic etiology related to SCLC and breast cancer, while GAD antibodies are more common in nonparaneoplastic disorders. GlyR antibodies may be present in stiff-person syndrome but are more frequent in PERM.
keyPoints: Amphiphysin antibodies indicate paraneoplastic etiology linked to SCLC and breast cancer.
GAD antibodies often occur in nonparaneoplastic disorders.
Treatment includes therapy of the underlying tumor, glucocorticoids, and drugs enhancing GABA-ergic transmission (e.g., diazepam, baclofen).
IVIg and plasma exchange may be transiently effective; rituximab and CD19 CAR T-cell therapy are options for nonresponsive patients.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY
items.title: Recognize Paraneoplastic Sensory Neuronopathy: Clinical Features and Management
items.content: Paraneoplastic sensory neuronopathy or dorsal root ganglionopathy presents with symmetric or asymmetric sensory deficits, painful dysesthesias, radicular pain, and decreased or absent reflexes. Electrophysiologic studies show decreased or absent sensory nerve potentials with normal motor conduction velocities. The disorder is immune-mediated, targeting the dorsal root ganglia, leading to neuronal loss and degeneration of spinal cord posterior columns. It often precedes encephalomyelitis and autonomic dysfunction, associated with Hu antibodies and SCLC.
keyPoints: Symptoms include sensory deficits, painful dysesthesias, radicular pain, and decreased reflexes.
Electrophysiologic studies show decreased or absent sensory nerve potentials.
Immune-mediated process targets dorsal root ganglia, causing neuronal loss.
Therapeutic approach focuses on tumor treatment; glucocorticoids may stabilize or improve symptoms.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.title: Identifying Paraneoplastic Peripheral Neuropathies: Key Features and Associations
items.content: Paraneoplastic peripheral neuropathies can manifest at any stage of cancer, with early-stage neuropathies showing rapid progression and inflammatory features. Late-stage neuropathies are often milder due to axonal degeneration and may be masked by chemotherapy neurotoxicity.
keyPoints: Neuropathies in early cancer stages show rapid progression, inflammation, and can have demyelinating features.
Late-stage neuropathies present with mild to moderate sensorimotor deficits due to axonal degeneration.
Features suggesting paraneoplastic etiology include asymmetric symptoms, neuropathic pain, dysautonomia, spinal cord or nerve root involvement, and an axonal pattern in electrophysiological studies.
Hu antibodies suggest concurrent dorsal root ganglionitis; CRMP5 antibodies may be present.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of Paraneoplastic Peripheral Neuropathies
items.content: Paraneoplastic peripheral neuropathies generally have a poor response to treatment. However, if demyelinating features are present, treatments like IVIg, plasma exchange, or glucocorticoids may improve symptoms.
keyPoints: Treatment response is usually poor for paraneoplastic neuropathies.
Demyelinating features may respond to IVIg, plasma exchange, or glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.title: Peripheral Nerve Hyperexcitability and Paraneoplastic Associations
items.content: Neuromyotonia (Isaacs' syndrome) is characterized by muscle fiber hyperactivity, cramps, fasciculations, and stiffness. It can occur with or without cancer, but when paraneoplastic, it is associated with thymomas and SCLC.
keyPoints: Neuromyotonia presents with muscle cramps, twitching, stiffness, and spasms.
Electromyogram shows fibrillations, fasciculations, and myokymic discharges.
Caspr2 antibodies are often present in Morvan syndrome; many isolated cases are antibody negative.
Associated tumors include benign/malignant thymomas and SCLC.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY
items.title: Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy: Recognize rapid progression and cancer association
items.content: Patients with immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy present with myalgias, rapid weakness in extremities, neck, pharyngeal, respiratory, and sometimes cardiac muscles. Elevated serum muscle enzymes and muscle biopsy showing extensive necrosis are key diagnostic features. The presence of antibodies against HMGCR is associated with a higher likelihood of underlying cancer compared to those with signal recognition particle antibodies.
keyPoints: Rapid progression of weakness involving extremities, neck, pharyngeal, respiratory, and cardiac muscles
Elevated serum muscle enzymes
Muscle biopsy shows extensive necrosis with minimal or absent inflammation
Antibodies against HMGCR are associated with a higher likelihood of underlying cancer
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES
items.title: Paraneoplastic visual syndromes: Identify retinopathy and optic neuritis in cancer patients
items.content: Paraneoplastic visual syndromes involve the retina, uvea, and optic nerves. Cancer-associated retinopathy presents with photosensitivity, vision loss, color perception issues, scotomas, night blindness, and ERG changes. SCLC is commonly associated. Melanoma-associated retinopathy involves acute night blindness and photopsias, with specific ERG findings. Paraneoplastic optic neuritis and uveitis may be linked to CRMP5 antibodies.
keyPoints: Photosensitivity, progressive vision loss, color perception issues, central or ring scotomas, night blindness
ERG shows attenuation of photopic and scotopic responses in cancer-associated retinopathy
SCLC is the most commonly associated tumor with paraneoplastic visual syndromes
Melanoma-associated retinopathy presents with acute onset of night blindness and photopsias
CRMP5 antibodies may be present in patients with paraneoplastic optic neuritis and uveitis
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which type of immune response is primarily involved in paraneoplastic neurologic disorders (PNDs) associated with antibodies against intracellular antigens?
options: Direct antibody effects on target antigens
items.explanation: PNDs associated with antibodies against intracellular antigens are primarily mediated by cytotoxic T-cell mechanisms.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of PNDs associated with intracellular antigens?
options: Extensive infiltrates of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells
Direct antibody effects on cell-surface antigens
Absence of neuronal antibodies in serum or CSF
items.explanation: PNDs associated with intracellular antigens are characterized by extensive infiltrates of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which type of PNDs is more responsive to immunotherapy?
options: PNDs associated with antibodies against intracellular antigens
PNDs associated with antibodies against cell-surface antigens
PNDs occurring in the terminal stages of cancer
items.explanation: PNDs associated with antibodies to antigens expressed on the neuronal cell surface are more responsive to immunotherapy.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which of the following is a likely cause for some PNDs where antigens remain unknown?
options: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome associated with neuroblastoma or SCLC
Viral encephalitis such as herpes simplex virus
Neuropathies occurring in terminal stages of cancer
items.explanation: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome associated with neuroblastoma or SCLC is an example where the antigens are unknown.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.question: Which of the following is a common feature in the presentation of paraneoplastic neurologic disorders (PNDs)?
options: Symptoms usually appear after the tumor has been diagnosed.
The neurologic syndrome develops slowly over several years.
Prompt tumor control does not affect the neurologic outcome.
Symptoms often appear before a tumor is known.
items.explanation: It is common for symptoms to appear before the presence of a tumor is known.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.question: What percentage of paraneoplastic neurologic disorders (PNDs) involving the peripheral nervous system have detectable neuronal or neuromuscular junction antibodies?
items.explanation: Less than 20% of PNDs involving the peripheral nervous system have detectable neuronal or neuromuscular junction antibodies.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.question: What is a typical finding in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of patients with paraneoplastic neurologic disorders affecting the CNS or dorsal root ganglia?
options: Mild to moderate pleocytosis (<200 mononuclear cells, predominantly lymphocytes)
items.explanation: The CSF profile typically consists of mild to moderate pleocytosis (<200 mononuclear cells, predominantly lymphocytes).
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.question: Which antibodies are most commonly associated with paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis and focal encephalitis in patients with SCLC?
items.explanation: Patients with SCLC and paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis or focal encephalitis usually have Hu antibodies in serum and CSF.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.question: Which imaging modality is often used to uncover tumors in patients with paraneoplastic neuropathies when other tests are negative?
items.explanation: Combined CT and PET scans often uncover tumors undetected by other tests in patients with paraneoplastic neuropathies.

items.question: Which antibodies are associated with limbic, hypothalamic, and brainstem encephalitis?
items.explanation: Antibodies to Ma proteins are associated with limbic, hypothalamic, and brainstem encephalitis.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.question: Which paraneoplastic syndrome is predominantly associated with brainstem encephalitis and seminomas?
items.explanation: Kelch-like protein 11 antibodies are predominantly associated with brainstem encephalitis and seminomas.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.question: Which demographic is most commonly affected by encephalitis with NMDAR antibodies?
items.explanation: Encephalitis with NMDAR antibodies usually occurs in young women and children.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.question: What is the typical response rate to treatment for patients with anti-NMDAR encephalitis?
items.explanation: About 85–90% of patients have substantial neurologic improvement or full recovery.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.question: Which type of encephalitis is associated with abnormal movements in children after the resolution of herpes simplex infection?
items.explanation: Approximately 25% of patients with herpes simplex encephalitis develop autoimmune encephalitis associated with abnormal movements.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.question: What is a common initial misdiagnosis for anti-NMDAR encephalitis?
items.explanation: The syndrome may be misdiagnosed as neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.question: Which antibody-associated encephalitis is characterized by prominent seizures and status epilepticus often requiring a pharmacologically induced coma?
items.explanation: $ \gamma $-aminobutyric acid type A (GABA$_{A}$) receptor antibodies are associated with prominent seizures and status epilepticus.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.question: Which antibody-associated encephalitis is frequently associated with Hodgkin's lymphoma?
items.explanation: Encephalitis with metabotropic glutamate receptor 5 (mGluR5) antibodies is frequently associated with Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.question: Which antibody-associated encephalitis often presents with short-term memory loss, seizures, and hyponatremia?
items.explanation: Encephalitis with leucine-rich glioma-inactivated 1 (LGI1) antibodies often presents with short-term memory loss, seizures, and hyponatremia.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.question: Which antibody-associated encephalitis is associated with Morvan syndrome?
items.explanation: Morvan syndrome is associated with contactin-associated protein-like 2 (Caspr2) antibodies.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.question: Which HLA haplotypes are strongly associated with anti-IgLON5 disease?
items.explanation: Anti-IgLON5 disease shows a strong association with the HLA-DRB1*10:01 and HLA-DQB1*05 haplotypes.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.question: What is the typical response to immunotherapy in patients with anti-IgLON5 disease?
items.explanation: The response to immunotherapy in patients with anti-IgLON5 disease is poor.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.question: Which clinical feature is NOT typically associated with paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration?
items.explanation: Opsoclonus is rarely seen in paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.question: Which antibody-associated syndrome has a more responsive outcome to immunotherapy?
items.explanation: The cerebellar syndrome associated with mGluR1 antibodies is more responsive to immunotherapy.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD
items.question: Which of the following tumors is most frequently associated with opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome in children?
items.explanation: At least 50% of children with opsoclonus-myoclonus have an underlying neuroblastoma.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD
items.question: Which of the following antibodies is associated with opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome and may lead to muscle rigidity, laryngeal spasms, and autonomic dysfunction?
items.explanation: A small subset of patients with opsoclonus-myoclonus have Ri antibodies, which can lead to muscle rigidity, laryngeal spasms, and autonomic dysfunction.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD
items.question: Which of the following treatments is NOT mentioned as a response to opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome?
items.explanation: Cyclophosphamide is mentioned for treating children with opsoclonus-myoclonus, but not specifically as a response to the syndrome in adults.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY
items.question: Which antibody is most commonly associated with a paraneoplastic etiology of stiff-person syndrome?
items.explanation: Amphiphysin antibodies usually indicate a paraneoplastic etiology related to SCLC and breast cancer.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY
items.question: Which of the following treatments is NOT mentioned as part of the management for stiff-person syndrome?
items.explanation: Antibiotics are not mentioned in the treatment options for stiff-person syndrome.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY
items.question: What is the primary target of the immune-mediated process in paraneoplastic sensory neuronopathy?
items.explanation: The disorder targets the dorsal root ganglia, causing neuronal loss and secondary degeneration.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.question: Which antibodies are associated with concurrent dorsal root ganglionitis in paraneoplastic neuropathies?
items.explanation: Detection of Hu antibodies suggests concurrent dorsal root ganglionitis.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.question: What is a common feature that suggests the possibility of a paraneoplastic etiology in peripheral neuropathies?
options: Presence of neuropathic pain and dysautonomia
items.explanation: An asymmetric distribution of symptoms, presence of neuropathic pain and dysautonomia suggest a paraneoplastic etiology.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.question: Which type of cancer is most commonly associated with vasculitis causing painful distal axonal sensorimotor neuropathy?
items.explanation: Vasculitis of the nerve and muscle is predominantly associated with SCLC.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.question: Which treatment may improve symptoms in paraneoplastic neuropathies with demyelinating features?
options: IVIg or plasma exchange, or glucocorticoids
items.explanation: If demyelinating features predominate, IVIg, plasma exchange, or glucocorticoids may improve symptoms.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES
items.question: Which muscle enzyme is typically elevated in patients with immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy?
items.explanation: Serum muscle enzymes, such as creatine kinase, are elevated in immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES

items.question: Which antibody is more likely to be associated with an underlying cancer in immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy?
items.explanation: Patients with antibodies against HMGCR and seronegative patients are more likely to have an underlying cancer.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES
items.question: Which tumor is most commonly associated with cancer-associated retinopathy?
items.explanation: The most commonly associated tumor with cancer-associated retinopathy is small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC).
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: PNDs associated with antibodies against cell-surface antigens are primarily mediated by cytotoxic T-cell mechanisms.
items.explanation: These PNDs are mediated by direct antibody effects on target antigens, not cytotoxic T-cell mechanisms.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: In patients with cancer, immune checkpoint inhibitors can lead to neurologic immune-related adverse events that mimic PNDs.
items.explanation: Immune checkpoint inhibitors are associated in less than 3% of cases with neurologic immune-related adverse events similar to PNDs.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.statement: MRI findings in most paraneoplastic neurologic disorders are specific.
items.explanation: In most PNDs, the MRI findings are nonspecific.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.statement: A biopsy of affected tissue is often difficult to obtain and not specific for PND.
items.explanation: Although useful to rule out other disorders, the pathologic findings from a biopsy are not specific for PND.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.statement: Paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis and focal encephalitis are usually associated with SCLC.
items.explanation: These syndromes are often linked to small cell lung cancer (SCLC).

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.statement: Most types of paraneoplastic encephalitis and encephalomyelitis respond well to treatments aimed at removing antibodies against intracellular antigens.
items.explanation: These conditions usually respond poorly to such treatments.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.statement: Most patients with anti-NMDAR encephalitis have abnormal findings on brain MRI.
items.explanation: In about 65% of patients, the brain MRI is normal.

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.statement: AMPA receptor antibody encephalitis commonly affects middle-aged women with an underlying tumor.
items.explanation: Encephalitis with AMPA receptor antibodies affects middle-aged women and 70% have an underlying tumor.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.statement: Encephalitis with dopamine-2 receptor antibodies is frequently associated with cancer.
items.explanation: Encephalitis with dopamine-2 receptor antibodies is extremely rare and not associated with cancer.

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.statement: In encephalitis with LGI1 antibodies, about 40% of patients have thymoma.
items.explanation: Less than 5% of patients with LGI1 antibody-associated encephalitis have thymoma.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.statement: Anti-IgLON5 disease is commonly associated with cancer.
items.explanation: Anti-IgLON5 disease is not associated with cancer.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.statement: MOG antibody syndromes are typically seen in adults over the age of 60.
items.explanation: Most patients with MOG antibody-associated syndromes are children and young adults.

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD
items.statement: Most patients with opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome have neuronal antibodies.
items.explanation: Most patients do not have neuronal antibodies.

items.statement: Paraneoplastic myelitis is mainly associated with breast and lung carcinomas.
items.explanation: Paraneoplastic myelitis is mainly associated with breast and lung carcinomas.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC OPSOCLONUS-MYOCLONUS SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES OF THE SPINAL CORD

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY
items.statement: GlyR antibodies are most frequently associated with stiff-person syndrome.
items.explanation: GlyR antibodies may occur in some patients but are more frequently detectable in PERM.

items.statement: IVIg and plasma exchange have proven benefits in treating paraneoplastic sensory neuronopathy.
items.explanation: The benefit of IVIg and plasma exchange is not proven for this disorder.

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.statement: Guillain-Barré syndrome and brachial plexitis have a clear paraneoplastic association with Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.explanation: There is no clear evidence of a paraneoplastic association between Guillain-Barré syndrome, brachial plexitis, and Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.statement: Peripheral nerve hyperexcitability can occur without cancer.
items.explanation: The disorder often occurs without cancer, although it may be paraneoplastic.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES
items.statement: Paraneoplastic retinopathies often show substantial improvement after treatment of the tumor and immunotherapy.
items.explanation: Paraneoplastic retinopathies rarely show substantial improvement; however, stabilization of symptoms and partial responses have been reported.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES
items.statement: Melanoma-associated retinopathy is characterized by reduced b-waves with normal dark adapted a-waves in the ERG.
items.explanation: The ERG shows reduced b-waves with normal dark adapted a-waves in melanoma-associated retinopathy.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: Most target antigens of high-risk antibodies in PNDs are intracellular proteins.
items.reason: High-risk antibodies have a greater than 70% probability of being associated with an underlying tumor.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why most target antigens of high-risk antibodies are intracellular.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: PNDs associated with antibodies against cell-surface antigens often involve extensive T-cell infiltrates.
items.reason: These PNDs are mediated by cytotoxic T-cell mechanisms.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these PNDs are mediated by direct antibody effects, not cytotoxic T-cell mechanisms.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA
items.assertion: The presence of antineuronal antibodies can help in the diagnosis of all paraneoplastic neurologic disorders.
items.reason: Only 60-70% of CNS PNDs and <20% of peripheral nervous system PNDs have detectable neuronal or neuromuscular junction antibodies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because not all PNDs have detectable antineuronal antibodies.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · Paraneoplastic Neurologic Disorders | PND OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AND DORSAL ROOT GANGLIA

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.assertion: The main concern in treating paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis should be to treat the tumor.
items.reason: Immunotherapies aimed at cytotoxic T-cell responses are considered important after addressing the tumor.
items.explanation: Treating the underlying tumor is crucial, and immunotherapy can complement this approach.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.assertion: CRMP5 antibodies are frequently associated with paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis.
items.reason: Detection of CRMP5 antibodies suggests a strong link to these syndromes.
items.explanation: CRMP5 antibodies are limited in their diagnostic usefulness for paraneoplastic neuropathies.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PND OF NERVE AND MUSCLE | PARANEOPLASTIC ENCEPHALOMYELITIS AND FOCAL ENCEPHALITIS WITH HIGH-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.assertion: Encephalitis with NMDAR antibodies can be misdiagnosed as a primary psychiatric disease.
items.reason: Some patients are initially evaluated by psychiatrists due to the presentation of severe psychiatric symptoms.
items.explanation: The reason is correct because initial psychiatric evaluation occurs due to symptom presentation.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER
items.assertion: Autoimmune encephalitis post-herpes simplex is usually more responsive to immunotherapy than anti-NMDAR encephalitis.
items.reason: This disorder develops a few weeks after the viral infection has resolved and is associated with new synthesis of antibodies against NMDAR.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; autoimmune encephalitis post-herpes simplex is usually less responsive to immunotherapy than anti-NMDAR encephalitis.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · ENCEPHALITIDES WITH INTERMEDIATE- OR LOW-RISK ANTIBODIES FOR AN UNDERLYING CANCER

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.assertion: Encephalitis with Caspr2 antibodies is associated with Morvan syndrome.
items.reason: Morvan syndrome includes clinical features of encephalitis, autonomic alterations, and peripheral nerve hyperexcitability or neuromyotonia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the association.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 1)
items.assertion: Encephalitis with neurexin 3 alpha antibodies has distinctive clinical features.
items.reason: The experience with this disorder is limited, and it does not appear to be associated with cancer.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; encephalitis with neurexin 3 alpha antibodies does not have distinctive clinical features.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 1)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.assertion: Anti-Yo antibodies in paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration are associated with breast or gynecologic cancers.
items.reason: Anti-Yo antibodies typically result in prominent or pure cerebellar degeneration, often linked to these types of cancers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: A (part 2)
items.assertion: The cerebellar syndrome associated with GAD65 antibodies responds well to immunotherapy.
items.reason: GAD65 is an intracellular synaptic protein, and its associated cerebellar symptoms have a poor response to treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the response to immunotherapy for GAD65-associated cerebellar syndrome is poor.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · A (part 2)

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY
items.assertion: Symptoms of stiff-person syndrome improve with sleep and general anesthetics.
items.reason: Electrophysiologic studies demonstrate continuous motor unit activity in stiff-person syndrome.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why symptoms improve with sleep or anesthetics.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC STIFF-PERSON SYNDROME | PARANEOPLASTIC SENSORY NEURONOPATHY OR DORSAL ROOT GANGLIONOPATHY

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.assertion: Peripheral nerve hyperexcitability is characterized by spontaneous muscle fiber activity.
items.reason: Clinical features include cramps, muscle twitching, and stiffness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES
items.assertion: Paraneoplastic autonomic neuropathy is always associated with cancer.
items.reason: It occurs in association with several tumors, including SCLC and lymphoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because paraneoplastic autonomic neuropathy may occur without cancer.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · PARANEOPLASTIC PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES
items.assertion: Patients with immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy may have an underlying cancer.
items.reason: Antibodies against HMGCR and seronegative patients are more likely to have an underlying cancer than those with antibodies against signal recognition particle.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why some patients may have an underlying cancer.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Neurologic Syndromes and Autoimmune Encephalitis · MYASTHENIA GRAVIS | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY | PARANEOPLASTIC VISUAL SYNDROMES
